title: Why Are Coding Bootcamps Dying? The Shift Back to Computer Science Degrees
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Five years ago, coding bootcamps were everywhere. The pitch was compelling: skip the four-year degree, spend twelve weeks learning to code, and land a six-figure job at a tech company. Stories of bootcamp graduates getting hired at Google and Amazon made the rounds. University computer science programs looked slow, expensive, and outdated by comparison. Then the tech job market shifted. Bootcamp placement rates dropped. Some bootcamps closed entirely. The conversation in tech forums has swung back toward the value of a traditional computer science degree. Here is what happened, why recruiters are prioritizing degrees again, and what it means if you are deciding between a bootcamp and a CS major.

What Changed in the Tech Job Market

The bootcamp boom coincided with a historic hiring frenzy in tech. Between 2015 and 2022, demand for software engineers far exceeded supply. Companies were hiring anyone who could pass a coding interview. A bootcamp certificate plus a strong portfolio was often enough to get an entry-level position. The market was forgiving. Employers were willing to train on the job because they needed bodies in seats.

Then the market cooled. Major tech companies conducted layoffs. Hiring slowed. The number of applicants per open position increased dramatically. In a tight market, employers become more selective. When a single job posting receives hundreds of applications, recruiters look for ways to filter the pile. A bachelor's degree in computer science is an easy filter. It signals a certain baseline of knowledge. It signals commitment. It reduces the risk of a bad hire. The bootcamp certificate, which was sufficient when demand was high, is less competitive when employers can afford to be picky.

What Bootcamps Teach, and What They Do Not

Bootcamps are designed to teach practical, job-ready skills as quickly as possible. A typical bootcamp covers web development frameworks, version control, and enough programming to build a functional application. Graduates can write code. They can build things. What they often cannot do is reason about algorithms, understand system design, or work with the theoretical foundations of computer science.

These gaps matter in a competitive market. The coding interview process at many tech companies still emphasizes data structures and algorithms, topics that are covered extensively in a CS degree and often skimmed or skipped in bootcamps. A bootcamp graduate might be able to build a web application but struggle with the technical interview required to get the job building web applications.

Bootcamps also provide less depth. Twelve weeks is enough to learn a specific stack of technologies. It is not enough to develop the adaptability that comes from studying computing principles. A CS graduate who learned operating systems, compilers, and computer architecture can pick up a new programming language or framework relatively quickly because they understand the underlying principles. A bootcamp graduate who learned React and Node.js may struggle when the job requires Python and Django.

When a Bootcamp Still Makes Sense

Bootcamps are not obsolete. They are just no longer the obvious shortcut they appeared to be. A bootcamp can still be a good choice in specific circumstances. If you already have a bachelor's degree in another field and are looking to pivot into tech, a bootcamp may be faster and cheaper than a second degree. If you have been programming on your own for years and need a credential and some structured learning to fill gaps, a bootcamp can serve that purpose. If you are targeting a specific role that a bootcamp specializes in placing graduates into, and the bootcamp publishes transparent, verified placement data, it may still be worth the investment.

The key is to research the specific bootcamp and its outcomes. Ask for placement data that is audited by a third party. Ask for the names of employers who have hired recent graduates. Contact recent graduates on LinkedIn and ask about their experience. A bootcamp with strong employer relationships and a track record of placement is very different from a bootcamp that is struggling to place graduates in a tighter market.

The Case for the CS Degree

A computer science degree provides three things a bootcamp generally cannot. First, it provides theoretical depth. Algorithms, data structures, computer architecture, operating systems, and theory of computation are not just academic exercises. They are the foundation on which practical skills are built. They make you adaptable. They prepare you for the hardest technical interviews. They give you a framework for understanding new technologies rather than just using them.

Second, a CS degree provides breadth. You are exposed to areas of computing you might never encounter in a bootcamp: databases, networks, security, artificial intelligence, human-computer interaction. This breadth helps you find your interests and makes you versatile in the job market. You might enter the degree thinking you want to be a web developer and leave wanting to work on compilers or robotics.

Third, a CS degree provides access to internships and on-campus recruiting. University career fairs bring employers to you. Internship programs are structured pipelines to full-time offers. These pathways are much harder to access as a bootcamp graduate navigating the job market alone.

The Bottom Line

Coding bootcamps are not dying because they are worthless. They are dying because the market conditions that made them an easy path to a high-paying job have changed. In a tighter market, the depth, breadth, and structured recruiting pipelines of a computer science degree are harder to replicate in twelve weeks. A bootcamp can still make sense for career changers with existing degrees or for self-taught programmers who need a credential. For most students starting from scratch, the CS degree is the stronger foundation. The shortcut is not as short as it used to be.
